literal: the inner instrument; from antar 'within' + karaṇa 'instrument'
invariant_definition: The internal cognitive instrument — the seat of mental functions, distinguished from the external sensory instruments (*bāhya-karaṇas*). In Vedānta psychology *antaḥkaraṇa* is fourfold: *manas* (the coordinating-doubting function), *buddhi* (the determining-decisive function), *citta* (the memory-/dwelling function), *ahaṅkāra* (the I-making function). All schools accept this fourfold structure; Sāṅkhya's tripartite scheme (*manas*, *buddhi*, *ahaṅkāra*) is sometimes preserved. Schools differ on the ontological status of *antaḥkaraṇa* — *vyāvahārika* (Advaita) vs. real *prakṛti*-evolute (Viśiṣṭādvaita, Dvaita).

per_school.definition: *Antaḥkaraṇa* is the *upādhi* of the *jīva* — the limiting condition through which the one *ātman* appears as multiple *jīvas*. Its *vṛtti*s (modifications) are the substance of cognition; the *akhaṇḍākāra-vṛtti* is the special *vṛtti* that takes *brahman* as undivided whole-object. The *antaḥkaraṇa* is *vyāvahārika*; in *brahma-jñāna* its individuating function ceases.

*Antaḥkaraṇa* is a real *prakṛti*-evolute through which the soul's *dharmabhūta-jñāna* operates. It contracts and limits *jñāna* under *karma*; at *mokṣa* it is dropped and *dharmabhūta-jñāna* operates without obstruction.
*Antaḥkaraṇa* is a real *prakṛti*-evolute distinct from the *jīva* itself. The *jīva*'s *svarūpa* is preserved across embodiments; what changes is its *antaḥkaraṇa*-association.

translator_note: 'Mind' is too coarse — *antaḥkaraṇa* is the entire inner-instrument complex, of which *manas* is one part. 'Inner organ' or 'inner instrument' (literal) is preferable. The Anglo-American 'mind' has no precise Sanskrit equivalent.
